turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Thomas has the opinion of the Court this morning, Case 23-1259, Blom Bank v. Honickman.

turns.name: CLARENCE THOMAS
turns.text: This case comes to us on the writ of certiorari to the United States Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 60(b) allows a district court to grant relief from a final judgment in enumerated circumstances. Rule 60(b)(6) is a catchall provision that permits district courts to provide such relief only in extraordinary circumstances. Petitioners, respondents here, are victims and families of victims of terrorist attacks carried out by Hamas. They sued Petitioner Blom Bank, alleging that it had aided and abetted Hamas' commission of the attacks. The District Court dismissed the complaint on the ground that plaintiffs had failed to state a claim, and the Second Circuit affirmed. The plaintiffs then moved under Rule 60(b)(6) to vacate the District Court's final judgment so that they could file an amended complaint. The District Court denied their motion, holding that they failed to demonstrate the extraordinary circumstances necessary to receive relief. The plaintiffs appealed again, and this time the Second Circuit reversed. The court held that when a party seeks vacatur under Rule 60(b) in order to file an amended complaint, a District Court must balance Rule 60(b)'s focus on finality against Rule 15(a)'s liberal repleading standard. In an opinion filed with the clerk today, we reverse. Relief under Rule 60(b)(6) requires extraordinary circumstances. That standard does not become less demanding when a Rule 60(b)(6) movement also hopes to amend his complaint. Rather, a party seeking to reopen his case must first satisfy Rule 60(b) on its own terms before Rule 15(a)'s liberal amendment standard can apply. Because the Second Circuit's approach conflates the order of operations and dilutes Rule 60(b)(6)'s standard, we disagree with its approach. For these reasons, and others set forth in our opinion, we reverse the judgment of the Second Circuit. Justice Jackson has filed an opinion concurring in part and concurring in the judgment.
